items.q: Can a device ever be 100 percent efficient?
items.correct: In practice, no — some energy is always transferred to less useful stores (e.g. heat to surroundings).
items.misconception: A well-designed device can be 100 percent efficient by eliminating all friction.
items.random_wrong: Efficiency above 100 percent is possible if the device extracts ambient energy.
items.why: Students think 'inefficiency' is a design flaw to be removed, missing that the second law of thermodynamics ensures some dissipation.

items.q: What is the difference between energy and power?
items.correct: Energy is measured in joules; power is the rate of energy transfer, measured in watts (joules per second).
items.misconception: Energy and power are the same — both describe how much fuel something uses.
items.random_wrong: Power is the energy used over the lifetime of a device.
items.why: Students use 'power' in everyday language to mean 'a lot of energy', missing that power has the dimension of energy per time.

items.q: When energy is 'lost' as heat due to friction, what has really happened?
items.correct: Energy has been transferred to the thermal store of the surroundings — it's not destroyed, just dissipated.
items.misconception: Energy has been used up and no longer exists.
items.random_wrong: Energy has been converted into mass following E=mc^2.
items.why: Everyday language ('used up', 'lost') and bills ('energy used') reinforce the idea energy is consumed, contradicting conservation.

items.q: A motor takes in 200 J and does 50 J of useful work. What is its efficiency?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 25%. “10%” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which is the best way to reduce energy wasted as heat in a moving machine?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Adding lubrication. “Painting it black” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A kettle uses 90 000 J to heat water, of which 72 000 J go into the water. What is its efficiency, as a percentage?
items.why: Exact answer: 80. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
